narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away feels like a door slamming shut just as you're reaching for the handle. The prediction tool we use looked at your profile and estimated a 60.7% chance of not passing the bar exam down the line, which is why we had to make this tough call. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 1, placing you at the very bottom of your class academically during that critical phase, and it was the strongest signal pulling the prediction toward failure. Layered on top of that, your LSAT score of 31 signaled some challenges in the kind of analytical reasoning the bar demands, making it the next biggest factor tipping the scales. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also played a big role, showing early struggles that set a pattern the tool couldn't ignore. Even with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.2 and upper-middle family income, those weren't enough to counterbalance, as they both nudged toward concerns about sustaining top performance under pressure. Your background as a white female and full-time student status added subtle weights in the same direction, though your gender was the one aspect that gently pulled toward success. It was this combination—like puzzle pieces forming a picture of inconsistent academic momentum—that painted a risky path ahead for the bar. You're clearly driven, with a full-time commitment and a respectable UGPA, but the tool saw those lower deciles and LSAT as flashing warning lights from students who've walked similar roads. This isn't the end of your story; many have climbed from here by targeting those weak spots, like boosting test prep or seeking targeted academic support. We truly wish you the best—law needs voices like yours, and with focus, you can rewrite this prediction. Keep pushing forward.
